verses.text: Now these be the last words of David. David the son of Jesse said, and the man who was raised up on high, the anointed of the God of Jacob, and the sweet psalmist of Israel, said, The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue. Guidance: The chapter opens with a formal oracle. Coming right after the long song of chapter 22, the editor pairs two poetic summaries of David's life — one looking back, one looking forward. These are 'the last words of David' in a literary, not chronological, sense — not the last sentences he ever spoke, but the final theological testament he wished to leave. The superscription is a small portrait: 'David the son of Jesse' (his ordinary origin); 'the man who was raised up on high' (his exaltation by God); 'the anointed of the God of Jacob' (his royal-priestly office); 'the sweet psalmist of Israel' (n'im zemiroth Yisrael — 'the pleasant one of the songs of Israel,' an exquisite title for the king-poet who shaped the worship of his people). Then the assertion that frames everything that follows: 'The Spirit of the LORD spake by me, and his word was in my tongue.' David claims direct prophetic inspiration for what he is about to say. The chapter is making him not merely a king who happened to write poems, but a prophet through whom the LORD spoke. The New Testament reads David exactly this way (Acts 2:30 calls him a prophet).

The God of Israel said, the Rock of Israel spake to me, He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God. And he shall be as the light of the morning, when the sun riseth, even a morning without clouds; as the tender grass springing out of the earth by clear shining after rain. Guidance: The oracle proper — David's distilled theology of kingship. 'The God of Israel said, the Rock of Israel spake to me.' Double designation; double witness. The content: 'He that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God.' Two non-negotiable qualifications for the just ruler. (1) Tzaddiq — righteous, doing justice in concrete cases. (2) Yir'at Elohim — the fear of God, an inner orientation that constrains the use of power. The ruler who lacks either fails the office. Then the consequence-imagery: 'as the light of the morning, when the sun riseth, even a morning without clouds; as the tender grass springing out of the earth by clear shining after rain.' Two parallel images. The just ruler is like sunrise on a cloudless morning — clarity, warmth, the start of a working day. He is like the green shoots that appear after rain — life springing up because the conditions are right. The chapter is offering us the inverse of Saul's reign and the corrective to David's own failures: when the king rules justly under God, the land flourishes. The image is agricultural — the green grass after rain — because in the ancient Near-East, fertility and good government were one indivisible blessing. Christians read these lines as ultimately fulfilled in Christ, the perfectly just King whose reign brings the morning of the new creation.

Although my house be not so with God; yet he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure: for this is all my salvation, and all my desire, although he make it not to grow. But the sons of Belial shall be all of them as thorns thrust away, because they cannot be taken with hands: But the man that shall touch them must be fenced with iron and the staff of a spear; and they shall be utterly burned with fire in the same place. Guidance: Two contrasting halves. (1) David admits failure: 'Although my house be not so with God' — his own dynasty has not in fact lived up to the just-ruler ideal he just sketched. He has Bathsheba on his record; his sons have committed rape, murder, rebellion. Yet — 'yet he hath made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things, and sure.' The Hebrew berith olam — 'everlasting covenant' — refers back to chapter 7. The Davidic covenant is 'ordered in all things' (arukah ba-kol — set up in every detail) and 'sure' (shemurah — guarded, preserved). David is locating his hope outside himself. He has not earned the covenant by his life; he relies on God's faithfulness to His own word. 'This is all my salvation, and all my desire' — the king's entire confidence rests on the LORD's covenant promise. (2) Then the contrast: 'the sons of Belial' (worthless men, scoundrels) 'shall be all of them as thorns thrust away.' Thorns are too prickly to grab with bare hands — you need iron tools to deal with them. The chapter is teaching us that the just-ruler ideal of v3-4 has a flip side: the unjust man, the man of Belial, will be dealt with as one deals with thorn-brush — gathered by iron implements and burned. The image is severe; in the messianic reading, the King who is the light of morning is also the King who deals decisively with evil. Two faces of the same sovereign justice.

These be the names of the mighty men whom David had: The Tachmonite that sat in the seat, chief among the captains; the same was Adino the Eznite: he lift up his spear against eight hundred, whom he slew at one time. Guidance: The roster of the gibborim — the mighty men — begins. After the high poetry of the 'last words,' the chapter pivots to a list of the warriors who made David's reign possible. The shift in genre is striking: from theology to commando-stories. The point is that David's dynasty was not built by his own efforts alone — it was built with these specific men, named one by one. The chapter wants us to know that the LORD's purposes work through human collaborators. The first name: 'The Tachmonite that sat in the seat, chief among the captains; the same was Adino the Eznite.' The Hebrew here is textually difficult (1 Chr 11:11 has slightly different details — Jashobeam, Hachmonite, 300 slain). The Septuagint reads 'Ish Bosheth' rather than 'sat in the seat'; modern editions often emend to 'Josheb-basshebeth.' Whichever reading is correct, the man is the chief — first among David's elite three. The number '800 slain with a spear at one time' is hyperbolic and may include the broader engagement, not solo combat; ancient war-rosters used such numbers to mark a warrior's stature. The chapter is establishing the man's reputation: he is the most distinguished combat-veteran in David's service.

And after him was Eleazar the son of Dodo the Ahohite, one of the three mighty men with David, when they defied the Philistines that were there gathered together to battle, and the men of Israel were gone away: He arose, and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword: and the LORD wrought a great victory that day; and the people returned after him only to spoil. Guidance: The second of the three: Eleazar son of Dodo. His story is concrete and visceral. The Israelites had retreated from a Philistine engagement; Eleazar stood his ground. 'He arose, and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword.' The detail is unforgettable — the muscles in his sword-hand cramped so completely that the sword had to be pried out of his fingers after the battle. He had been fighting so long his body had welded to the weapon. 'The LORD wrought a great victory that day' — the narrator credits the LORD, not Eleazar. The man's heroism was the channel, not the source. 'The people returned after him only to spoil' — the Israelite army returned not to fight (Eleazar had already won) but only to collect plunder. The chapter is portraying a hero whose stand turned a rout into a victory; the credit is to the LORD; the practical effect was that the demoralized army got to come back. There is a model here of how spiritual leadership often works: one man stands when others flee; the LORD vindicates him; later the broader community returns to share in what his faithfulness made possible.

And after him was Shammah the son of Agee the Hararite. And the Philistines were gathered together into a troop, where was a piece of ground full of lentiles: and the people fled from the Philistines. But he stood in the midst of the ground, and defended it, and slew the Philistines: and the LORD wrought a great victory. Guidance: The third of the elite three: Shammah son of Agee. His story echoes Eleazar's. A Philistine raiding party came to the Israelite farmland; the men of Israel ran away (an interesting picture of how routine survival-fear of the Philistines had become); Shammah alone stood in the middle of a lentil field and defended it. The detail 'a piece of ground full of lentiles' is touching. The plot was not strategic — it was a vegetable patch. A lentil-field was somebody's food for the season. Shammah refused to let it be trampled. He stood in the middle and fought off the raid. 'The LORD wrought a great victory' — the same line as Eleazar's story; the narrator wants the parallel registered. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's victories happen not only over kings and giants but over field-raiders. The thing being defended is ordinary; the courage to defend it is extraordinary; the LORD honors it as 'a great victory.' There is dignity in the small fight. The mighty-men list is consistently theological — every act of courage is credited to the LORD, every action is a hinge on which the LORD chose to act, but the human courage is named, dated, located, remembered.

And three of the thirty chief went down, and came to David in the harvest time unto the cave of Adullam: and the troop of the Philistines pitched in the valley of Rephaim. And David was then in an hold, and the garrison of the Philistines was then in Bethlehem. And David longed, and said, Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, which is by the gate! And the three mighty men brake through the host of the Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate, and took it, and brought it to David: nevertheless he would not drink thereof, but poured it out unto the LORD. And he said, Be it far from me, O LORD, that I should do this: is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives? therefore he would not drink it. These things did these three mighty men. Guidance: The famous water-from-the-well-of-Bethlehem story — one of the most loved episodes in 2 Samuel. The setting: David is in the cave of Adullam (his old wilderness refuge, during a Philistine occupation); the Philistines have garrisoned Bethlehem itself; David is cut off from his hometown. In a moment of nostalgic longing he says aloud, perhaps to no one in particular, 'Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, which is by the gate!' He is not issuing a command; he is voicing a memory — the cold sweet water of the village well of his boyhood. But three of the gibborim hear it. They take it as a command — or more, as a chance to serve their king. They cut through the Philistine garrison, draw water from the Bethlehem well, fight their way back, and bring David the cup. David's response is the heart of the story. He refuses to drink. 'Be it far from me, O LORD, that I should do this: is not this the blood of the men that went in jeopardy of their lives?' He pours the water out as a libation to the LORD — too precious to drink, because three men had risked their lives for it. The chapter is recording the king's moral imagination at its best. He receives the costly gift, recognizes its true value (the blood-cost), and consecrates it. Christians have read this passage devotionally for centuries as a parable: every gift purchased at great cost should be received with the reverence due to blood-bought things. The supreme application is the cross — what was bought at the price of Christ's life is too precious for casual use.

And Abishai, the brother of Joab, the son of Zeruiah, was chief among three. And he lifted up his spear against three hundred, and slew them, and had the name among three. Was he not most honourable of three? therefore he was their captain: howbeit he attained not unto the first three. Guidance: Abishai — the brother of Joab and one of the most active warriors of David's reign. He has appeared throughout 2 Samuel: ready to kill Saul in the cave (1 Sam 26), ready to kill Shimei on the road (2 Sam 16), leader against the Ammonites (2 Sam 10), rescuer of David from the giant Ishbi-benob (2 Sam 21). Here his record is summarized: he 'lifted up his spear against three hundred, and slew them.' He is the chief of a second tier — the chief of a 'three' below the original three of v8-12. The narrator carefully distinguishes between the elite-three (Josheb-basshebeth, Eleazar, Shammah) and Abishai's tier. Abishai is 'most honourable of three' (his subgroup) but 'attained not unto the first three.' The chapter is laying out the actual hierarchy of military reputation in David's army. The detail matters because honors-of-war were taken seriously; the inheritance of David's elite veterans would have depended on which tier they occupied. The narrator is preserving institutional memory.

And Abishai, the brother of Joab, the son of Zeruiah, was chief among three. And he lifted up his spear against three hundred, and slew them, and had the name among three. Was he not most honourable of three? therefore he was their captain: howbeit he attained not unto the first three. Guidance: Abishai — the brother of Joab and one of the most active warriors of David's reign. He has appeared throughout 2 Samuel: ready to kill Saul in the cave (1 Sam 26), ready to kill Shimei on the road (2 Sam 16), leader against the Ammonites (2 Sam 10), rescuer of David from the giant Ishbi-benob (2 Sam 21). Here his record is summarized: he 'lifted up his spear against three hundred, and slew them.' He is the chief of a second tier — the chief of a 'three' below the original three of v8-12. The narrator carefully distinguishes between the elite-three (Josheb-basshebeth, Eleazar, Shammah) and Abishai's tier. Abishai is 'most honourable of three' (his subgroup) but 'attained not unto the first three.' The chapter is laying out the actual hierarchy of military reputation in David's army. The detail matters because honors-of-war were taken seriously; the inheritance of David's elite veterans would have depended on which tier they occupied. The narrator is preserving institutional memory.

And Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man, of Kabzeel, who had done many acts, he slew two lionlike men of Moab: he went down also and slew a lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow: And he slew an Egyptian, a goodly man: and the Egyptian had a spear in his hand; but he went down to him with a staff, and plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear. These things did Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and had the name among three mighty men. He was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three. And David set him over his guard. Guidance: Benaiah — captain of David's bodyguard, later promoted by Solomon to commander of the army (1 Kings 2). His exploits are three. (1) 'Two lionlike men of Moab' — the Hebrew arieli Moav, possibly meaning 'sons of Ariel' (proper name) or 'lion-like warriors.' Either reading celebrates a fight where Benaiah killed two formidable Moabite champions. (2) 'A lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow.' This is the most picturesque detail in the entire roster. Snow is rare in Israel; a lion had fallen into (or been trapped in) a pit; Benaiah climbed down into the snowy pit and killed the lion. The image suggests a man who actively sought out the dangerous fight rather than avoiding it. (3) An Egyptian giant — 'a goodly man' (the Hebrew suggests size) — armed with a spear; Benaiah with only a staff disarmed him and killed him with his own spear. The David-and-Goliath echo is unmistakable; Benaiah replicated David's signature feat. The summary: 'He was more honourable than the thirty, but he attained not to the first three.' Benaiah is in the second tier alongside Abishai. 'David set him over his guard' (mishma'at — bodyguard, or possibly 'audience' — those who had personal access to the king). The chapter is showing us the man who would become the executioner-enforcer of Solomon's accession — a warrior whose courage David had personally tested and trusted.

Asahel the brother of Joab was one of the thirty; Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem, Shammah the Harodite, Elika the Harodite, Helez the Paltite, Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, Abiezer the Anethothite, Mebunnai the Hushathite, Zalmon the Ahohite, Maharai the Netophathite, Heleb the son of Baanah, a Netophathite, Ittai the son of Ribai out of Gibeah of the children of Benjamin, Benaiah the Pirathonite, Hiddai of the brooks of Gaash, Abialbon the Arbathite, Azmaveth the Barhumite, Eliahba the Shaalbonite, of the sons of Jashen, Jonathan, Shammah the Hararite, Ahiam the son of Sharar the Hararite, Eliphelet the son of Ahasbai, the son of the Maachathite, Eliam the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite, Hezrai the Carmelite, Paarai the Arbite, Igal the son of Nathan of Zobah, Bani the Gadite, Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Beerothite, armourbearer to Joab the son of Zeruiah, Ira an Ithrite, Gareb an Ithrite, Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all. Guidance: The full roster of the Thirty — actually thirty-seven, with the final tally noted at the end. The names roll past quickly, but several deserve note. (1) 'Asahel the brother of Joab' — the swift-footed son of Zeruiah whom Abner killed at Gibeon in 2 Sam 2; the roster honors a dead man, suggesting the list was compiled (or at least preserved) early in David's reign. (2) 'Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem' — possibly the slayer of one of the giants from chapter 21. (3) 'Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite' — from the same village as Amos and the wise woman of chapter 14. (4) 'Eliam the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite.' This name stops the reader: Eliam, son of Ahithophel. Ahithophel was David's brilliant traitor-counselor who hanged himself after siding with Absalom (chs 15-17). Eliam was also Bathsheba's father (2 Sam 11:3). The roster shows that one of David's elite Thirty was Bathsheba's father, which provides an additional human context for the Bathsheba scandal: David committed adultery with the wife of one of his own captains' families. (5) 'Uriah the Hittite: thirty and seven in all.' The roster ends with Uriah's name. The very man David murdered to cover the affair with Bathsheba is the last name on the list of his most loyal warriors. The narrator does not comment, but the placement is devastating. The chapter that began with the soaring 'last words' on the just-ruler covenant ends, quietly and damningly, with the name of the man David betrayed. The whole book of 2 Samuel is, in the end, the story of a great king and his great sin; the editor will not let us close the catalog without remembering both.
